stories.headline: Broncos acquire Dolphins wide receiver Jaylen Waddle in blockbuster trade
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have agreed to acquire Miami Dolphins wide receiver Jaylen Waddle in a trade, ending the team's quiet opening to free agency with a major move. Denver is sending its first-round pick (No. 30 overall), a third-round pick (No. 94), and a fourth-round pick (No. 130) to Miami in exchange for Waddle and a fourth-round selection (No. 111).

Waddle, 27, recorded 910 yards and six touchdowns in the 2025 season and had a career-best 1,356-yard campaign in 2022. General manager George Paton began serious discussions with Miami's new front office regime following the NFL Combine earlier this month, with talks intensifying in the final 48 hours before the deal was struck.

The acquisition addresses a significant need at wide receiver that was exposed during the Broncos' AFC Championship Game loss to New England, when injuries to Troy Franklin and Pat Bryant left Denver thin at the position. Waddle is expected to travel to Denver on Tuesday to complete a physical before the trade is made official.

The deal marks the first time Denver has surrendered a first-round pick since trading two for quarterback Russell Wilson in 2022. The Broncos' earliest pick in the upcoming draft is now No. 62, though the team is projected to hold 10 picks in the 2027 draft.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis activates Colorado Drought Task Force as record warmth and low snowpack worsen conditions statewide
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis activated Colorado's Drought Task Force on Tuesday as record-breaking warmth and historically low snowpack pushed more than half the state into extreme drought conditions. The task force was last activated in 2020, the year of several catastrophic wildfires including the East Troublesome fire, the third-most destructive in Colorado history.

Polis also activated Phase 2 of the state's drought response plan, directing the Water Conservation Board to continue monitoring snowpack, streamflow, soil moisture, and reservoir storage. If conditions deteriorate further, the state would advance to Phase 3, which triggers an official drought declaration.

Colorado Department of Natural Resources Executive Director Dan Gibbs said his agency will play a central role in developing short-, medium-, and long-term drought response recommendations for the governor. Statewide snow water equivalent currently ranks among the lowest in more than four decades, according to the governor's office.

"Colorado is experiencing the warmest year so far in our 131-year record, and one of the driest," Polis said in a statement. The task force includes the Departments of Natural Resources, Agriculture, Local Affairs, and Homeland Security and Emergency Management.

stories.headline: Three ballot measures on transgender sports, gender-affirming surgery, and child sex trafficking qualify for November vote
stories.summary:
Colorado voters will decide in November whether to ban transgender children from participating in sports aligned with their gender identity, whether to prohibit gender-affirming surgeries for minors, and whether to make child sex trafficking punishable by life in prison without parole. The three measures were placed on the ballot after the conservative group Protect Kids Colorado collected more than 125,000 voter signatures for each initiative.

Initiative 110, the final of the three measures, received signature approval from state elections officials on Tuesday. The initiatives join two measures already on the November ballot dealing with immigration enforcement cooperation and increased fentanyl penalties.

Rocky Mountain Equality, an LGBTQ rights nonprofit, called initiatives 109 and 110 "an attack on Colorado families modeled after national extremist efforts" and is working to oppose them under the banner of the issue committee Families Not Politics. Protect Kids Colorado executive director Erin Lee said the measures "empower everyday Coloradans to take action, protect children, and restore common-sense policies."

Voters may also face additional tax-related questions on the November ballot. The qualification of these measures sets up a significant culture-war flashpoint in a state that has in recent years trended toward expanding LGBTQ protections.

stories.headline: Colorado AI policy working group reaches unanimous agreement on framework for regulating automated decision-making
stories.summary:
The Colorado AI Policy Work Group reached a unanimous agreement Tuesday on a policy framework governing the use of artificial intelligence and automated decision-making systems in consequential decisions affecting consumers. The group, convened by Gov. Polis, includes representatives from consumer organizations, hospitals, school districts, and technology companies both large and small.

The recommendations must still be translated into legislation and pass the Colorado General Assembly during the current session before becoming law. The effort stems from Colorado's landmark 2024 AI law — the first of its kind in the nation — which Polis signed reluctantly and which prompted formation of the working group to refine its implementation.

"I am very grateful to the hardworking members of the Colorado AI Policy Working Group that have reached a unanimous agreement on AI policy to protect consumers and support innovation in our state," Polis said in a written statement. The framework addresses protections for consumers while attempting to preserve conditions favorable to technology industry growth in Colorado.

stories.headline: Colorado legislature advances social media warrant bill, citing Evergreen High School shooting
stories.summary:
A bipartisan bill requiring social media companies to respond more quickly to law enforcement search warrants passed the Colorado House on Monday and awaits a final procedural vote in the Senate before heading to Gov. Polis. Senate Bill 26-011 would require platforms with at least one million monthly Colorado users to acknowledge receipt of warrants and, in most cases, provide the requested information within three days.

Legislators and law enforcement officials have pointed to last September's shooting at Evergreen High School — where a 16-year-old seriously wounded two students before killing himself — as a case where faster social media cooperation could have enabled intervention. The Anti-Defamation League had tipped the FBI months before the shooting about an account discussing mass violence plans, but investigators said the relevant social media company was slow to respond.

The bill also requires platforms to establish law enforcement hotlines. It is sponsored by a bipartisan team including Sens. Lisa Frizell and Dylan Roberts and Reps. Andrew Boesenecker and Anthony Hartsook, and passed the House with near-unanimous support.

Polis vetoed a broader version of the bill last year that included additional provisions on firearms sales, illicit substances, and child exploitation material. This year's narrower version focuses exclusively on warrant response timelines and has broad stakeholder support from law enforcement groups and Children's Hospital Colorado.

stories.headline: Mayor Johnston appoints former Brownstein partner Emily Garnett as Denver's chief operations officer
stories.summary:
Denver Mayor Mike Johnston has appointed former Brownstein Hyatt Farber Schreck partner Emily Garnett as the city's chief operations officer, a senior leadership role within his administration. Garnett will oversee operations of the mayor's office and city agencies under his purview, and will coordinate matters with the Denver City Council.

In her new permanent role, Garnett will be responsible for managing citywide goals and improving permitting efficiency, two priorities Johnston has emphasized during his tenure. "I'm honored to become a permanent member of the Mayor's team and support the incredible public servants," Garnett said.

stories.headline: Denver-based Re/Max and Kentwood Real Estate join Zillow's new pre-market listings platform
stories.summary:
Denver-based Re/Max and Kentwood Real Estate are among the first real estate firms to partner with Zillow on a new digital platform for pre-market, or "coming soon," home listings. The move places the two Colorado firms at the forefront of an intensifying national competition over access to properties before they hit the open market.

The battle over pre-market listings has grown more competitive as national brokerages seek to capture buyer interest earlier in the home-search process. The Zillow partnership gives Re/Max and Kentwood clients visibility on one of the country's most widely used real estate platforms before a property is formally listed.

stories.headline: Colorado quantum computing hub launches with $14 million campus and workforce training program
stories.summary:
A new quantum computing hub has launched in Colorado, anchored by a $14 million campus and an accompanying workforce training program aimed at filling a growing talent gap in the emerging technology sector. The initiative comes as the field faces a significant labor shortage, with an estimated three open positions for every qualified job candidate nationally.

Colorado has positioned itself as a hub for quantum technology research and commercialization, with federal laboratories in the Boulder and Denver metro areas playing key roles in the sector's development. The new facility is intended to accelerate both research and the pipeline of trained workers needed to staff quantum computing companies.

stories.headline: MacKinnon calls out Avalanche coaches after 7-2 loss, team hosts Stars on Wednesday with Central Division lead shrinking
stories.summary:
Colorado Avalanche captain Nathan MacKinnon publicly criticized the team's coaching staff and defensive structure Monday following a 7-2 home loss to the Pittsburgh Penguins, saying the team's "flow is off" and that players need clearer communication before games. MacKinnon said the use of seven defensemen has disrupted chemistry and line combinations across the roster.

"Lines and defensive pairs need to sit down and go through clips and figure out what our system is right now," MacKinnon told reporters. "Too much passing around the perimeter. We don't shoot enough. We haven't had 30 shots in four games now. Which is very, very rare for us."

Coach Jared Bednar acknowledged the performance was unacceptable, saying players were "getting beat, getting out-competed" and giving up easy scoring chances — problems he said were addressed after a similar performance against Edmonton but resurfaced Monday. The loss tightened Colorado's lead in the Central Division to three points after Dallas fell to Utah on the same night.

The Avalanche host the Dallas Stars on Wednesday at Ball Arena in a pivotal divisional matchup. Colorado's Western Conference-leading position, which had appeared comfortable, has become more precarious following losses in Winnipeg and at home to Pittsburgh.

stories.headline: Man fatally shot in Denver's Park Hill neighborhood; homicide investigation underway
stories.summary:
A man was shot and killed Tuesday morning in Denver's Park Hill neighborhood, police said. Officers responded to the 12400 block of East 42nd Avenue at 11:43 a.m. and found the victim with a gunshot wound; he was later pronounced dead at a hospital.

Denver police are investigating the death as a homicide and said they are working to develop information about a suspect. No arrest has been announced. Anyone with information is asked to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers at 720-913-7867.

stories.headline: Denver police seek public help locating 14-year-old girl missing since early March near Ruby Hill Park
stories.summary:
Denver police are asking for the public's help locating 14-year-old Ariiss Muniz, who was last seen on March 2 near South Navajo Street and West Jewell Avenue in the area between the Ruby Hill and College View/South Platte neighborhoods. She was last observed at approximately 7 a.m.

Ariiss is described as 4 feet, 11 inches tall and 107 pounds, with brown hair and brown eyes. Anyone with information about her whereabouts is asked to call Denver police at 720-913-2000 and reference case number 26-117475.

stories.headline: DCPA announces 2026-2027 Broadway season featuring 'Death Becomes Her,' 'The Great Gatsby,' and more
stories.summary:
The Denver Center for the Performing Arts announced its 2026-2027 Broadway season on Tuesday, highlighted by the national tour of "Death Becomes Her" and Tony Award-winning productions including "A Beautiful Noise: The Neil Diamond Musical" and "Maybe Happy Ending." The season also includes "The Great Gatsby" and returning favorites such as "The Book of Mormon" and "Disney's Beauty and the Beast."

"This season underscores the elements that define Broadway at its best — spectacle, storytelling, and the profound emotional journeys that only live theatre can provide," said DCPA Broadway and Cabaret Executive Director John Ekeberg. Three of the five nominees for Best Musical at the 2025 Tony Awards are included in the lineup.

Shows will be staged primarily at the Buell Theatre and other venues within the Denver Performing Arts Complex. The season runs from fall 2026 through fall 2027, with tickets currently available to subscribers and single tickets to go on sale to the public at a later date.

weather.notable: About 25 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: You ever eat so much you feel sick? And then you eat more? I call that 'dessert.'

topStory.headline: Illinois Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton Wins Democratic Senate Primary, Defeating Two Incumbent Congressmembers
topStory.summary:
Illinois Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton won the Democratic primary for the U.S. Senate seat being vacated by retiring Sen. Dick Durbin, defeating Reps. Raja Krishnamoorthi and Robin Kelly, NBC News and the Associated Press projected. Stratton led with approximately 40 percent of the vote in Cook County, beating Krishnamoorthi by 20 points in the city of Chicago, with roughly 80 percent of the expected vote counted.

Stratton's victory came despite a significant financial disadvantage against her primary opponents. Krishnamoorthi, a five-term House member and prolific fundraiser, spent $29 million on advertisements, while Stratton's campaign spent approximately $1.1 million. The gap was partially offset by Illinois Future PAC, funded by Democratic Gov. JB Pritzker, which spent $14.9 million boosting Stratton and attacking Krishnamoorthi. Stratton also withstood nearly $10 million in attack ads from Fairshake, a super PAC backed by cryptocurrency industry executives.

Stratton, a South Side Chicago native, ran as the most progressive candidate in the field, calling for Medicare for All, a $25 hourly minimum wage, and the abolition of ICE. She received endorsements from Pritzker, Sen. Tammy Duckworth, and Sen. Elizabeth Warren. In her victory remarks, Stratton said, "Tonight we showed what's possible when you listen to the people and give the people what they want." She has also stated she will not support Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer in a future leadership vote.

The race carries historic significance. If Stratton wins the general election in November, she would become the sixth Black woman ever to serve in the U.S. Senate and would mark the first time three Black women served in the chamber simultaneously. Stratton enters the general election as a heavy favorite in Illinois, a reliably Democratic state. Durbin's Senate seat has not been contested in an open race since 1996.
